coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and separation performances of composite membranes containing copper ferrite photocatalysts on graphene oxide nanosheets
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Hai-Ming
Zhu, Li-Jing
Sun, Yang-Yang
coredata.dc:description: An innovative approach thatintegrates photocatalysis, adsorption, and membrane separation processes was presented to degrade pollutants simultaneously during the filtration process. Firstly, a one-pot method was employed to prepare 3D graphene oxide/carbon quantum dot/copper ferrite (G/CFQ) photocatalysts. Then, the M−G/CFQ membrane was fabricated by intercalating 3D G/CFQ photocatalysts between 2D graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets using a negative pressure filtration technique. The time-dependent flux and MB residual rate results revealed that the M−G/CFQ membrane exhibited superior photocatalytic capabilities, particularly in alkaline environments. Furthermore, M−G/CFQ, when used without reaching adsorption saturation and under illumination, displayed outstanding long-term operational catalytic degradation capabilities. For a 5 ppm methylene blue (MB) solution at pH of 7, 10, and 12, the filtrate residue rates were only 8.63 %, 4.44 %, and 1.61 % respectively within 6 h. The versatility, stability, and exceptional properties of M−G/CFQ make it a promising candidate for large-scale water treatment applications, contributing significantly to the conservation of clean water resources and environmental sustainability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic membrane
CQD
CuFe2O4
Dynamic photocatalytic filtration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide and CQD
Characterization of CQD, G/CF and G/CFQ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesize process of CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQD. (c) PL emission spectrum of CQDs aqueous solution. Insets are optical photographs under asunlight lamp (left) and UV excitation (right).
XRD patterns of G/CF and G/CFQ.
TEM images and electron diffractions of G/CF (a, b, c, d) and G/CFQ (e, f, g, h).
The element mapping images of C, N, O, Fe, and Cu for G/CF and G/CFQ.
Photos and SEM images of M−G/CFQ (a, b, c) and M−G/CF (d, e, f). (g) Cross-section SEM image of M−G/CFQ. (h) Illustration of the assembly of GO and G/CFQ sheets and the transfer channels.
The dynamic contact angles of (a) M−G/CF and (b) M−G/CFQ.
(a) The device to detect the degradation efficiency of the membrane. (b) The degradation efficiency of M−G/CF and M−G/CFQ.
(a) Zeta potential of G/CFQ. (b) The adsorption capacity of G/CFQ for MB in the solution of pH at 3, 7, 10 and 12.
The time-dependent flux and MB residual rate of M−G/CFQ under simulated sunlight with MB solution of pH (a) 3, (b) 7, (c) 10, and (d) 12.
(a) The static degradation efficiency of M−G/CFQ. The time depending flux and MB residual rate of M−G/CFQ for MB of (b)10 ppm and (c) 5 ppm.
(a) The static degradation efficiency of M−G/CFQ. The time-dependent flux and MB residual rate of M−G/CFQ under simulated sunlight of pH (b) 7, (c) 10, and (d) 12.
(a) Photograph and UV–vis spectra of water before and after immersed M−G/CFQ for 8 days. (b) The time-depending flux and MB residual rate of M−G/CFQ.
(a) XPS spectra of M−G/CFQ. Core-level XPS spectra of (b) O 1s, (c) Fe 2p and (d) Cu 2p.
(a) Photocatalytic mechanism schematic of M−G/CFQ. (b) DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra of •OH in aqueous dispersion. (c) The degradation efficiency of M−G/CFQ under different conditions.
The schematic diagram of the photocatalysis composite membrane.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and separation performances of composite membranes containing copper ferrite photocatalysts on graphene oxide nanosheets

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Marine Materials
Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative approach thatintegrates photocatalysis, adsorption, and membrane separation processes was presented to degrade pollutants simultaneously during the filtration process. Firstly, a one-pot method was employed to prepare 3D graphene oxide/carbon quantum dot/copper ferrite (G/CFQ) photocatalysts. Then, the M−G/CFQ membrane was fabricated by intercalating 3D G/CFQ photocatalysts between 2D graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets using a negative pressure filtration technique. The time-dependent flux and MB residual rate results revealed that the M−G/CFQ membrane exhibited superior photocatalytic capabilities, particularly in alkaline environments. Furthermore, M−G/CFQ, when used without reaching adsorption saturation and under illumination, displayed outstanding long-term operational catalytic degradation capabilities. For a 5 ppm methylene blue (MB) solution at pH of 7, 10, and 12, the filtrate residue rates were only 8.63 %, 4.44 %, and 1.61 % respectively within 6 h. The versatility, stability, and exceptional properties of M−G/CFQ make it a promising candidate for large-scale water treatment applications, contributing significantly to the conservation of clean water resources and environmental sustainability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Membrane separation techniques developed based on the unique selective separation capability, have been practically applied in multiple fields [1–3]. The complexity and diversity of separation environments, coupled with the vast array of separation substances, pose a significant challenge for the separation membrane field [4,5]. In addition, when the separation membrane contact with the pollutant in various environments, it is inevitable that the trapped substances will contact with the membranes, especially under the pressure drive, leading to membrane pollution, reducing their effective size and weakening the separation efficiency [1,6,7].

Membrane pollution is a complex issue due to various factors. To address these challenges, ongoing research and development are focused on the design and optimization of separation membranes [8–11]. Recently, active antifouling strategies have been proposed by introducing antimicrobial agents onto membranes to kill bacteria by interfering with biochemical pathways [12]. Based on antimicrobial agents, active antifouling strategies provide a powerful tool to prevent and control fouling. By carefully selecting the appropriate strategy and antimicrobial agent, it is possible to create surfaces that are resistant to fouling while minimizing environmental impact. Catalina et al. [13] hypothesized the active antifouling mechanism by exploring the specific interaction between Ag NPs and bacterial cells. The Ag NPs were found to disrupt the cell wall and membrane, causing leakage of intracellular contents and ultimately leading to cell death. Additionally, the NPs interacted with cellular proteins and enzymes, interfering with their normal functions. This dual mode of action made the Ag NPs highly effective against a wide range of bacterial species. Crystalline TiO2 NPs were also employed in constructing active antifouling membrane surfaces used to self-assemble onto polyamide layer [14–16] or introduced onto PVDF membranes [17–19] to improve the antibacterial properties. In addition, for non-biological pollutants, such as dyes and antibiotics, the introduction of degradation-specific catalysts during the separation process offers a highly efficient solution for their removal. These catalysts are designed to trigger decomposition reactions that break down the harmful compounds into harmless or less toxic substances [20–22].

Graphene oxide (GO) has been widely used in membrane separation fields in recent years due to its unique two-dimensional (2D) sheet structure, designability, and unique oxygen-functionalized groups [23–26]. GO nanosheets, known for their exceptional mechanical properties and high surface area, provide excellent support for the catalysts, ensuring their stability and durability during the filtering process. The suitable region between the nanosheets acts as a catalyst carrier, maximizing the contact area between the catalyst and pollutants, thereby enhancing the catalytic efficiency [27–31]. Xie et al. [32] successfully fabricated a graphene oxide/MIL-88A(Fe) membrane utilizing a straightforward vacuum filtration technique. The effective intercalation of the photo-Fenton catalytic M88A within the GO nanosheets significantly enhanced the membrane's separation efficiency and flux, leveraging synergistic separation/photo-Fenton processes. Liu et al. [33] introduced triethanolamine (TEOA) modified titanate nanowires (TNWs) into GO membranes to enhance the membrane photocatalytic properties. The modified membrane exhibited exceptional photocatalytic degradation capabilities towards Congon Red dyes and membrane fouling was significantly mitigated. However, it is noteworthy that the presence of excessive catalyzer particles during the film-making process can potentially compromise the integrity of the nanosheet structure of GO. Furthermore, the formation of heterostructures may contribute to membrane instability over extended periods of operation. Consequently, addressing the challenges associated with catalyst immobilization, enhancing membrane stability, and optimizing the removal efficiency of dye pollutants remain crucial issues that require urgent attention and resolution.

In this manuscript, CQD and spinel structural catalyst copper ferrate (CuFe2O4) were first loaded onto graphene nanosheets. Then the photocatalytic membrane M−G/CFQ was fabricated by sandwiching the loaded catalyst between graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets using negative pressure filtration, as shown in Scheme 1 . The enhanced optoelectronic capture capability of copper ferrite (CuFe2O4) due to the addition of carbon quantum dots (CQD) played a pivotal role in improving the overall photocatalytic performance of the membrane. To investigate the photocatalytic degradation mechanism of M−G/CFQ, a series of experiments were conducted and M−G/CFQ exhibited remarkable static and dynamic photocatalytic degradation abilities. The membrane offers a promising solution for efficient and sustainable water treatment applications. Future studies could focus on optimizing the membrane structure and exploring its potential for treating a wider range of pollutants.

Natural graphite (350 meshes), ethylenediamine monohydrate, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, CH3COONa, citric acid (CA), methylene blue (MB), methyl violet (MV), Rhodamine B (RB) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. The polypropylene (PP) membrane with a mean pore size of 10 μm was bought from https://www.taobao.com. KMnO4, H2O2, H2SO4, K2S2O8, P2O5, NaOH, HCl, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide and CQD
ce:section.ce:para: GO nanosheets were synthesized by the modified Hummer method as reported [34]. The concentration of the GO solution was measured by theweighting method.

To prepare CQD, 5.225 g CA, and 1.785 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water. After stirring for 30 min, the mixed solution was transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted for 5 h under 180 ℃. CQD was obtained by dialyzing 24 h with adialysis bag (MWCO: 1000) to remove the impurities [35].

Briefly, 0.16 g Cu (NO3)2·3H2O, 0.52 g Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 0.52 g CH3COONa, and 0.032 g PVP (k30) were added into 50 mL of deionized water and mixed thoroughly. Then, 0.1 g CQD and 10 mL of graphene oxide solution (4 g/L) were added into the solution and stirred for 1 h. Afterward, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Finally, the obtained solids were centrifugally cleaned with deionized water five times, and the centrifuged product was freeze-dried for 48 h to obtain the G/CFQ. In comparison, catalyst G/CF without CQD was prepared under the same reaction conditions.

0.03 g G/CFQ and 10 mL GO solution (0.1 g/L) were dispersed in deionized water and stirred for 2 h. Then, the dispersion was filtered onto thePP porous membrane by a vacuum pump under 1 bar. The M−G/CFQ membrane was obtained after another 2 h compaction under the pressure after the dispersion was all passed. In comparison, the M−G/CF membrane was prepared using a mixture of G/CF and GO under the same reaction conditions.

The fluoroluminescence properties of CQD were determined using a fluorescence spectrometer (FL3-111, France). Surface morphology was measured by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S4800, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100, Japan) images were obtained with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) for the determination of sample morphology and element mapping. The XRD patterns were obtained on a D8 Advance Davinci (Bruker) diffractometer. Water contact angle (WCA) was measured by a contact angle measuring system (OCA20, Data Physics, Germany). The absorbance spectra were measured via a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, Lambda 950, US). Free radicals were measured by anElectron-spin paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, EPR E500, Germany).

The permeability and Fenton's reagent activating capability of the membranes, when utilizing 10 parts per million (ppm) concentration of dye as the influent, were meticulously determined at an applied pressure of 0.9 bar. The steady flux (J0 , L m-2 h- 1) was calculated according to our previous report [36,37]. The concentration of dyes was measured by UV spectrometer (Lambda 950, American), and the residual rate (R, %) can be calculated via the following equation:
(1)
R =
C 1
C 0
× 100 %
where C1 and C0 are the concentration of dye in the filtrate and feed solution, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQD, G/CF and G/CFQ
ce:section.ce:para: CQD was synthesized via a hydrothermal process using ethylenediamine and CA as carbon sources. As seen in Fig. 1 a, CA and ethylenediamine formed polymer-like CDs by reacting with citric acid and ethylenediamine and then carbonized to form the CDs. Furthermore, the synthetic CQDs particles show alarge number of carboxyl, making the CQDs have the potential to anchor on the GO surface. The synthesized CQD was characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence emission spectroscopy. As seen in Fig. 1b, CQDs have strong absorption in the ultraviolet region and it also could extend to thevisible region, the characteristic peaks at 239 and 343 nm are ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 conjugate domains and n-π* electron transitions dominated by surface oxygen-containing functional groups, respectively [38]. In addition, the photoluminescence of CQDs can be seen in Fig. 1c, the prepared CQDs solution appears reddish brown when exposed to sunlight, but shows blue fluorescence color under anultraviolet lamp, andthe CQDs solution appears bright blue (illustrated in Fig. 1c). This indicates that CQD has obvious photoluminescence characteristics and has a good ability to capture photoelectrons.

The structural characteristics of the G/CF and G/CFQ materials, which were produced through the synthesis process, are meticulously analyzed using X-ray Diffraction (XRD) techniques. As seen in Fig. 2 , a distinct peak is observed at an angle of 14.2°, which is identified as the signature of GO. This indicates that some of the GO has not yet undergone reduction, retaining its characteristic features. For G/CF, the XRD spectrum reveals a series of peaks that correspond to the Fe2O3 (Iron(III) Oxide) and CuO (Copper(II) Oxide) standards. This suggests that the synthesized materials are indeed composed of these specific compounds, Fe2O3 and CuO. When CQDs are introduced into the system, a noticeable transformation in the XRD spectrum of the G/CFQ material becomes apparent. The emergence of additional peaks at angles of 35.5°, 62.8°, and 74.4° represent (311), (440) and (533) for CuFe2O4 in the XRD spectrum of G/CFQ signifies the formation of new crystalline structures. This suggests that the CQDs have facilitated the growth of CuFe2O4 particles on the surface of the graphene sheets. The integration of CQDs into the system has thereby resulted in the transformation of the G/CF composite into a novel G/CFQ material with distinct crystallographic features, characterized by the presence of CuFe2O4 particles.

The microstructure and crystalline structure of G/CF and G/CFQ were observed by TEM (Fig. 3 ). As seen in Fig. 3, randomly distributed particles grown on the surface of lamellar reduced graphite oxide, and the folded morphology of reduced graphite oxide can be seen in TEM. In addition, it was found that without CQD, large compounds consisting of Fe2O3 and CuO were grown on the GO surface. For G/CFQ, the particle radius on graphene significantly decreased, andthe particle size decreased from the original 60–150 nm to 5–20 nm. From Fig. 3c, g, it can be discerned that the spacing between crystal planes for G/CF is measured at 0.266 nm, which aligns with the (104) crystal plane of Fe2O3 as calculated from XRD, which is represented by an angle of 33.1°. In contrast, for G/CFQ, the crystal plane spacing is observed to be 0.255 nm, which corresponds to the (311) crystal plane of CuFe2O4, as indicated by an angle of 35.8° in the XRD analysis. A detailed examination of the selected area electron diffraction patterns reveals that both G/CF and G/CFQ present polycrystalline structures. However, the electron diffraction pattern of G/CF is characterized by a scattered distribution, whereas the pattern of G/CFQ exhibits a discontinuous ring structure. This distinct feature further confirms the smaller size of the catalyst particles on G/CFQ. Prepared from ethylenediamine and sodium citrate, CQDs could provide a large number of amino groups onto GO, thus introducing a more active site on the surface of GO, inducing more CuFe2O4 particles onto G/CFQ.

Furthermore, TEM was used to determine the elemental distribution of G/CF and G/CFQ (Fig. 4 ). As seen, no N elements can be seen in G/CF, and the distribution of O, Fe, and Cu elements corresponds to the position of the particle. For G/CFQ, N elements are distributed uniformly onto the surface, attributing to the amino groups of CQDs. During the hydrothermal reaction, CQDs first reacted with GO, introducing macromolecular –OH and –COOH groups onto the nanosheet surface. When CuFe2O4 grows on the surface of rGO, the active site, as the reaction center, produces CuFe2O4 particles with smaller particle sizes. From Fig. 4, it can be seen that these small CuFe2O4 particles are relatively evenly distributed on the graphene sheet, thus the distribution of O, Fe, and Cu elements in the G/CFQ sample is also relatively uniform.

To build an M−G/CFQ photocatalytic composite membrane with nanochannel, the prepared sample sheets G/CFQ were fixed with GO and dispersed in deionized water, then fabricated by vacuum-assisted filtering G/CFQ and GO nanocomposite dispersion through the PP substrate. From the photos and SEM images of M−G/CFQ (as seen in Fig. 5 a,b,c), the top surface of the membrane is smooth and black, and a large number of protruding small particles can be seen in the surface SEM morphology of M−G/CFQ. For M−G/CF, fabricated by G/CF and GO with the same method, the surface particles are more obvious, showing a higher degree of surface irregularities. Under negative pressure, CFQ and GO spontaneous assembly to heterostructure GO-G/CFQ channels (Fig. 5d–h), G/CFQ and GO building layer by layer, forming a photocatalytic composite membrane. The dynamic contact angle was used to determine the surface, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . It is well known that the PP base membrane has a certain hydrophobic property, after G/CFQ assembly, the wettability of the membrane of the membrane surface is significantly reduced. The initial contact angle of M−G/CF is 76.9°, changing a little in 60 s. The initial contact angle of M−G/CFQ is reduced to 63° and then to 49° over 60 s. CQDs contains a large number of –OH and –COOH, which improved the hydrophilicity of the sample nanosheets. Therefore, although the catalyst particles on G/CFQ are smaller, resulting smoother membrane surface than G/CF, M−G/CFQ shows better wettability.

Firstly, astatic degradation experiment was applied to investigate the photocatalytic degradation performance of as-prepared M−G/CF and M−G/CFQ via the removal efficiency of model pollutants. As shown (Fig. 7 a), the prepared M−G/CF and M−G/CFQ were fixed in the dead-end diaphragm filtration, and 50 mL of MB solution (5 ppm) was added. After darkness for 60 min to establish adsorption equilibrium, the solution was degraded under the solar simulator (1.00 kW/m2) irradiation for 90 min. As seen from Fig. 7b, MB can be removed by the M−G/CFQ at about 60 min of Solar Simulator irradiation. However, M−G/CF showed abad photocatalytic degradation rate, the residual rate of MB in the solution is still 45.1 % after 90 min, indicating the better degradation property of M−G/CFQ. By comparison, to see, the photocatalytic composite membrane M−G/CFQ with CQD has a higher degradation efficiency, therefore, this membrane is selected for further dynamic degradation and separation experiments.

With the two-dimensional lamellar assemblage structure and the loaded CQD and CuFe2O4, M−G/CFQ has been designed as a comprehensive membrane integrated separation, adsorption, and catalysis into one. The photocatalytic capacity of the membrane is directly related to its adsorption capacity, for the catalytic reaction can be enhanced by the better pollutant adsorption capacity. For G/CFQ, the introduction of CQDs with amounts of –COOH and –OH, led to a lower zeta potential (−18.2 mV) (Fig. 8 a). The electronegativity of the samples provided a potential to adsorb positively charged dyes. The incorporation of CQDs into G/CFQ, which possess abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, predominantly promotes intermolecular interactions through hydrogen bonding and charge attraction between contaminants and G/CFQ [39,40]. Additionally, as an adsorbent material, iron-based organic frameworks (Fe-MOFs) exhibit remarkable characteristics such as a large specific surface area, ultra-high porosity, and easy modulation of component structure, leading to exceptional adsorption properties [41]. To determine the adsorption capacity of the M−G/CFQ, 0.3 g of G/CFQ was added to 200 mL of 10 ppm MB with pH of 3, 7, 10, and 12, stirring away from light for 24 h. The content of MB in the solution before and after adsorption was determined and the calculated corresponding adsorption rate was shown in Fig. 8b. As seen, with the increase of pH from 3 to 12, the MB adsorption capacity of G/CFQ showed a significant increase. The MB adsorption capacity increased from 6.44 mg/L to 85.36, 137.64 and 192.88 mg/L, respectively. It is known that the half-life of •OH radical in water is only 10-6 ∼ 10-9 s, and the ideal mass transfer distance is about 90 nm. Therefore, the better adsorption effect is beneficial to the catalyst anchor of the dye molecules [42]. It could reduce the mass transfer distance of •OH to the target pollutants, enhance the utilization rate of free radicals, and improve the photocatalytic Fenton reaction efficiency of the catalyst.

To further investigate the separation and photo-Fenton degradation performance of M−G/CFQ, simulated dyed wastewater was prepared with 5 ppm MB and H2O2 (0.1 %) in water and then passed through the membranes on the holder of the filter device under 0.9 bar. The MB solution was continuously filtered for 120 min under the dark field until the filtrate concentration was almost unchanged. Notable, MB solution was replenished quickly to ensure the constancy of level depth of 5 cm. Then, 1500 W m−2 simulated sunlight was applied to the membrane, and its photocatalytic separation and degradation performance for MB solutions with pH values of 3, 7, 10, and 12 were measured. As shown in Fig. 9 , little increase in flux can be seen for all these experimental data and achieve stability. The fluxes of M−G/CFQ for MB solutions at pH 3, 7, 10, and 12 after 100 min were 73.6, 71.7, 74.2, and 89.2 L m-2 h−1, respectively. The photo-Fenton degradation process occurred during the application of simulated sunlight. As depicted in Fig. 9a, the degradation efficiency of M−G/CFQ for MB solution at a pH of 3 was notably low (C/C0: 83.9 %). However, upon adjusting the pH value of the MB solution to 7, a marginal improvement in degradation capability was observed, resulting in a decrease in the residual MB rate to 52.4 % after 100 min. Notably, when the MB solution was alkaline, the degradation efficiency of M−G/CFQ was further significantly enhanced. Specifically, the MB residue rate in the filtrate decreased to 34.6 % and 7.8 % at solution pH levels of 10 and 12, respectively. Combining the separation and photo-Fenton degradation ability, M−G/CFQ could effectively remove the small molecular dyes at pH 10 under a large flux dynamic filtration process.

The pH value of the solution has a significant impact on the photo-Fenton degradation process of M−G/CFQ. When the pH is acidic (pH 3), the degradation efficiency is very low, which may be because the hydroxyl radicals generated by the photo-Fenton reaction are easily neutralized by hydrogen ions in the solution, reducing their reactivity. As the pH increases, the degradation efficiency improves gradually. This is because the hydroxyl radicals generated by the photo-Fenton reaction are more reactive in alkaline solutions, and can more effectively oxidize and degrade organic pollutants. In addition, the alkaline environment is also conducive to the formation of more hydroxyl radicals, further improving the degradation efficiency.

From the above discussion, it was found that the composite membrane with better properties could be obtained under alkaline conditions.To further explore the photo catalytic degradation ability of M−G/CFQ, experiments were conducted at different MB concentrations at pH 12. For the static degradation experiment, M−G/CFQ showed excellent performance in degrading MB. When the initial MB concentration was 5 ppm, the degradation efficiency of M−G/CFQ was close to 100 % after 10 min of reaction, while it requires 16 min for 10 ppm MB solution (Fig. 10 a), indicating that M−G/CFQ cloud rapidly degenerates the MB solution under static degradation experiment. However, for the dynamic filtration degradation process, the performance of M−G/CFQ was slightly reduced. At a filtration flux of about 80 L m-2 h−1, the residual rate of MB in the filtrate after 100 min of filtration was still 24.1 % (Fig. 10b), indicating the inadequate degradation capability for 10 ppm MB under the 80 L m-2h−1 filtration rate. In contrast, with almost the same filtration flux, the residual rate of MB in the filtrate for 5 ppm MB was 7.8 % (Fig. 10c). With higher MB concentration, better photocatalytic degradation ability was needed, the as-prepared M−G/CFQ cannot guarantee rapid degradation and removal of MB in the filtrate at a concentration of 10 ppm at a high flow rate.

The adsorption capacity of a membrane is crucial to its performance, as it directly affects the membrane's ability to separate materials efficiently. Membranes with high adsorption capacity can capture and retain a greater amount of dye molecules, resulting in improved separation and purification. The unadsorbed M−G/CFQ composite membrane was selected for direct photo catalytic filtration testing. Fig. 11 a shows the static degradation ability of the composite membrane at pH values of 7, 10, and 12. It can be seen that the composite membrane exhibits better degradation performance under alkaline conditions, with complete degradation of MB solution achieved within 15 min at pH 12. However, at pH 10, complete degradation requires 26 min, while neutral conditions require 60 min. The long-term dynamic photocatalytic filtration performance of the M−G/CFQ composite membrane is shown in Fig. 11b–d. As seen, at the beginning of filtration, the MB residue rate in the filtrate rapidly decreased. At pH 7 (Fig. 11b), the dye residue rate in the filtrate increased slowly with time, reaching 8.63 % after 6 h. In contrast, at pH 10 and 12, the alkaline solutions, the dye residue rates in the filtrate were only 4.44 % and 1.61 % after 6 h, respectively. This indicates that the M−G/CFQ composite membrane exhibits better long-term dynamic photocatalytic filtration performance under alkaline conditions.

In summary, the M−G/CFQ composite membrane exhibits good photocatalytic filtration performance, particularly under alkaline conditions. This is mainly due to the significant impact of membrane adsorption capacity on membrane performance. At the beginning of filtration, M−G/CFQ mainly adsorbs the dye, leading to a rapid decrease in the MB residue rate in the filtrate. With time, the combination of membrane separation, material adsorption, and catalytic degradation results in the membrane's treatment capacity for MB solution reaching a balance, and after 6 h of operation, the membrane still maintains excellent catalytic degradation ability.

To investigate the membrane stability, M−G/CFQ was immersed in pure water for 8 days. The water solution remained clear after 8 days and no obvious ultraviolet characteristic peak was present (Fig. 12 a). Thus, the M−G/CFQ was certified with good stability. In addition, to investigate the reused property of M−G/CFQ, the degradation ability of M−G/CFQ was retested after being immersed in water for 4 days and 8 days. Fig. 12b exhibits the real-time fluxes and MB residual rate of M−G/CFQ under simulated sunlight of pH 10. The flux reduces a little after three filtration cycles. Moreover, thedegradation ability of M−G/CFQ decreases a little but still maintains more than 95 %. Thus, the M−G/CFQ was certified with reused ability and recycled performance.

The surface composition and chemical state of M−G/CFQ were analyzed by XPS. As seen, M−G/CFQ was dominated by C, N, O, Fe, and Cu due to the successful loading ofCQDs and CuFe2O4 onto the 2D rGO (Fig. 13 a). In Fig. 13b, it can be seen that the peaks at 284.7, 286.0, 287.6, and 289.0 eV, which are ascribed to C–C, C=C, C-O/C-N, and C=O, proved convincingly the loading of CQDs. Therefore, –NH2, –OH, and –COOH are introduced onto the membrane. Fig. 13c shows the presence of six peaks in the M−G/CFQ composite at a binding energy of 710.4 eV and 712.9 eV for Fe 2p3/2, and 724.1 eV and 726.4 eV for Fe 2p1/2, which indicated the existence of Fe (II) and Fe (III) resulted from pure CuFe2O4 [43–46]. In addition, the peaks at 933.1 eV, 944.0 eV, and 953.1 eV can be assigned to Cu 2p3/2 from Cu(II) oxide and Cu(I), and Cu(II) from phthalocyanine, and Cu 2p1/2 from Cu(II) oxide [44–46], which is consistent with pure CuFe2O4 (Fig. 13d). The coexistence of Fe (II)/Fe (III) and Cu(I)/Cu(II) realized the valence cycle of Fe and Cu in the reaction center under photo Fenton conditions.

It is known that the spinel structure of CuFe2O4 has good catalytic degradation ability. The introduction of CQD with photoelectron capture ability could further enhance the catalytic performance of CuFe2O4. As shown in Fig. 14 a, during the filtration process, G/CFQ first captures and retains the MB molecule around the catalyst. Under the irradiation of sunlight, the CQD on G/CFQ is excited by light to produce electrons and holes, which react with Cu II and Fe III in copper ferrite to convert them into Cu I and Fe II. Cu I and Fe II can further react with H2O2 in the filtrate to generate •OH active radicals (Fig. 14b). The strong adsorption capacity of G/CFQ on MB reduces the mass transfer distance of free radicals diffusing to the target pollutant, enhances the utilization rate of free radicals, and ensures the efficient photocatalytic Fenton reaction of M−G/CFQ. Without adding H2O2, the degradation property of MB is obviously decreased (Fig. 14c). In conclusion, the excellent photocatalytic performance of the M−G/CFQ composite membrane is attributed to the combined effect of CQD, CuFe2O4 catalytic ability, and efficient adsorption of dyes. The efficient photocatalytic properties of M−G/CFQ can effectively reduce dye contamination of the membrane, achieve a self-cleaning effect during the separation process, and enable the composite membrane to have stable and efficient long-term separation and degradation ability.

This manuscript combines photocatalysis, adsorption, and membrane separation processes to synchronously degrade pollutants during the filtration process, achieving long-term and efficient separation. CQD was introduced to prepare catalysts with these properties and a one pot method was used to prepare G/CFQ photocatalysts loaded with CQD and CuFe2O4. M−G/CFQ was prepared by inserting 3D loaded catalysts between 2D graphene oxide nanosheets using thenegative pressure filtration method. And static degradation ability of M−G/CFQ and the dynamic photocatalytic filtration performance of M−G/CFQ at different pH values were explored. The unique photo generated electron properties of CQD enable M−G/CFQ to have better photocatalytic ability compared to M−CF. Under pH 12, the dye residue rates in the filtrate were only 1.61 % after 6 h with a flow rate of 89.2 L m-2 h−1, showing excellent long-term dynamic photocatalytic filtration performance under alkaline conditions. Besides, for the M−G/CFQ without adsorption saturation which isdirectly filtered under light, combined with the membrane separation, material adsorption, and catalytic degradation, M−G/CFQ exhibited excellent long-term operation catalytic degradation ability. The residue rate for the 10 ppm MB solution with pH 7,10 and 12, the filtrate was only 8.63 %, 4.44 %, and 1.61 % after 6 h of operation. The versatility, stability, and excellent property make M−G/CFQ a promising candidate for large-scale water treatment applications, contributing to the preservation of clean water resources and environmental sustainability.

Hai-Ming Song: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Resources. Li-Jing Zhu: Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yang-Yang Sun: Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the Talent-introduction Training Special Project of NBPT (No. NZ23RC07).
